county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Created 1911 from Nez Perce County. North-central Idaho county on the Camas Prairie. Named for Meriwether Lewis.
county_metadata.research_tips: Lewis County Recorder in Nezperce (the town) has deed records from 1911. Lewis County sits on the Camas Prairie, a productive wheat-farming region. The county is named for Meriwether Lewis of the Lewis and Clark expedition, which passed through in 1805. The Nez Perce Reservation partially overlaps Lewis County; tribal records and BIA records at NARA Seattle document enrollment and allotment. Craigmont, Winchester, and Kamiah-area communities have distinct settlement histories. Check Nez Perce County for pre-1911 records. The Lewis County Historical Society has pioneer family files. The Camas Prairie Railroad (a branch of the Northern Pacific) brought settlers; railroad land records at the Minnesota Historical Society document purchasers. Winchester Lake area homesteads are documented in BLM's GLO records. Norwegian and Swedish immigrant families are prominent in county records and Lutheran church registers.

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Grantor/grantee deed books from 1911 to present. County Recorder maintains index books and recorded instruments.
Marriage licenses and certificates from 1911. County Recorder issued licenses; state registration from 1947.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Created 1895 from Alturas County (extinct). South-central Idaho county. Parent county for several Magic Valley counties.
county_metadata.research_tips: Lincoln County Recorder in Shoshone holds deed records from 1895. Lincoln County was the parent county for Jerome and Gooding counties. Shoshone was an important Oregon Short Line railroad junction — railroad records at the Union Pacific Historical Collection and ISHS identify early residents. The Lincoln County Historical Society museum has pioneer family files. The Magic Valley irrigated farming community developed after the Milner Dam (1905); irrigation company records document early settlers. Alturas County (extinct 1895) records are at ISHS. Basque sheepherders worked the ranges north and south of the Snake River; sheep company records and Basque boarding house records survive at ISHS and the Basque Museum in Boise. Dietrich, Richfield, and other small communities have distinct settlement histories. The Shoshone Journal newspaper archives are on microfilm at ISHS and document the railroad and irrigation eras.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Created 1913 from Fremont County. Eastern Idaho county. Home to Brigham Young University-Idaho. Strong LDS settlement.
county_metadata.research_tips: Madison County Recorder in Rexburg has deed records from 1913. Rexburg is the home of Brigham Young University-Idaho (originally Ricks College, founded 1888 by the LDS Church) — university archives and alumni records provide genealogical data. LDS ward and stake records are the primary genealogical source for virtually all Madison County families; the Rexburg Stake (organized 1884) and its subordinate wards document every family in detail on FamilySearch. Check Fremont County for pre-1913 records. The Teton Dam collapse of June 5, 1976, devastated Rexburg and surrounding communities, killing 11 and destroying thousands of homes — disaster relief records, insurance claims, and FEMA files document affected families. The Madison County Historical Society has pre-flood and recovery-era family records. Sugar beet farming employed seasonal laborers. The Rexburg Standard Journal newspaper archives are on microfilm at ISHS.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Created 1913 from Lincoln County. South-central Idaho agricultural county. Site of the Minidoka War Relocation Center (WWII Japanese-American internment).
county_metadata.research_tips: Minidoka County Recorder in Rupert has deed records from 1913. The Minidoka Irrigation Project (1904-1906) opened thousands of acres to farming; Bureau of Reclamation records at NARA Denver document water rights holders and original settlers. The Minidoka War Relocation Center (Hunt Camp, 1942-1945), one of ten WWII Japanese-American internment camps, was located near Hunt/Eden on the Minidoka-Jerome county line; NARA holds extensive records including internee registration, property disposition, and camp administration files. The Minidoka National Historic Site preserves the camp. Check Lincoln County for pre-1913 records. The Minidoka County Historical Society museum in Rupert has pioneer family files and irrigation-era records. Rupert and Paul communities developed as irrigation towns. Sugar beet and potato farming employed Japanese, Filipino, and Mexican laborers.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Created 1864 as one of the original Idaho Territory counties. Lewiston was the first territorial capital (1863-1865). Named for the Nez Perce people.
county_metadata.research_tips: Nez Perce County Recorder in Lewiston holds deed records from 1864, among the oldest in Idaho. Lewiston was Idaho's first territorial capital (1863-1865) and the head of navigation on the Snake River. Territorial records from the Lewiston era are at ISHS in Boise. The Nez Perce people give the county its name; the Nez Perce National Historical Park headquarters is in Spalding, and the Nez Perce Reservation is partially within the county. BIA Nez Perce Agency records at NARA Seattle include census rolls (1885-1940), allotment records, and school files. Spalding Mission (est. 1836 by Henry Spalding) produced some of Idaho's earliest written records. The Luna House Museum and Nez Perce County Historical Society in Lewiston have pioneer family collections. Lewiston's Chinatown served Chinese miners and railroad workers in the 1860s-1880s. The Lewiston Tribune newspaper archives extend to 1892.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Created 1864 as one of the original Idaho Territory counties. Southeastern Idaho county. Early Mormon settlement. Parent county for much of southeastern Idaho.
county_metadata.research_tips: Oneida County Recorder in Malad City holds deed records from 1864, making it one of the oldest county record sets in Idaho. Oneida County was the parent county for much of southeastern Idaho — Bear Lake, Bannock, Franklin, Caribou, and Power counties were all carved from Oneida. Welsh and Mormon settlers established Malad City in the 1860s; the Welsh community left distinctive records including Welsh-language church documents. LDS ward records are the primary genealogical source for most Oneida County families, with the Malad Stake organized in 1888; records are indexed on FamilySearch. The Oneida County Pioneer Museum in Malad City has family files and settlement-era photographs. The Oregon Short Line railroad came through in 1878, connecting the isolated communities. Samaria, Stone, and Holbrook communities have distinct LDS ward records documenting every family.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Created 1864 as one of the original Idaho Territory counties. Idaho's largest county by area (prior to Idaho County taking that title with boundary changes). Remote southwestern Idaho. Duck Valley Indian Reservation (Shoshone-Paiute).
county_metadata.research_tips: Owyhee County Recorder in Murphy holds deed records from 1864, making it one of the oldest continuous record sets in Idaho. Owyhee County is vast and remote, covering Idaho's southwestern corner. Silver City was a major mining boomtown in the 1860s-1880s — mining district records, mine company records, and the Owyhee Avalanche newspaper (1865+) at ISHS document the community. The Duck Valley Indian Reservation (Shoshone-Paiute Tribes) straddles the Idaho-Nevada border; BIA Western Shoshone Agency records at NARA Pacific Region document tribal enrollment and allotment. The Owyhee County Historical Museum in Murphy has mining-era and ranching family files. Basque sheepherders worked extensively in Owyhee County; sheep camp records and boarding house registers survive. The county's extreme remoteness means early records are sparse outside Silver City. Chinese miners at Silver City and other camps left tax and census records. Jordan Valley (across the Oregon border) was linked to Owyhee County settlement.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Created 1917 from Canyon County. Western Idaho county at the confluence of the Payette and Snake Rivers. Agricultural community.
county_metadata.research_tips: Payette County Recorder in Payette holds deed records from 1917. Payette is at the confluence of the Payette and Snake Rivers on the Idaho-Oregon border. The Oregon Short Line railroad and later irrigation projects brought settlers. Apple and fruit orchards were a major industry — fruit growers' cooperative records at ISHS identify member families. Check Canyon County for pre-1917 records. The Payette County Historical Society museum has pioneer family files. New Plymouth was a planned agricultural colony founded in 1896 by settlers from Plymouth, Indiana — colony records and promotional materials document the founding families. Fruitland grew as an orchard community. BLM's GLO records document homestead entries. The Payette Independent-Enterprise newspaper archives are on microfilm at ISHS. Japanese-American farm families were present before WWII; some were interned at Minidoka.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Created 1913 from parts of Bingham, Blaine, and Oneida counties. Southeastern Idaho county. Strong LDS settlement.
county_metadata.research_tips: Power County Recorder in American Falls has deed records from 1913. American Falls was relocated when the original townsite was flooded by American Falls Dam (1927) — records of the town relocation at the Bureau of Reclamation, NARA Denver, and ISHS document affected families and property. The Massacre Rocks area along the Oregon Trail saw emigrant-Native conflicts documented in trail diaries and military records. LDS ward records are important for Power County families; wards organized in the 1890s-1900s have records on FamilySearch. Check Bingham, Blaine, and Oneida counties for pre-1913 records. The Power County Historical Society in American Falls has pioneer family files. The Fort Hall Indian Reservation borders the county; some BIA records cover cross-boundary families. Rockland and Arbon Valley communities have distinct LDS settlement histories.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Created 1864 as one of the original Idaho Territory counties. Home to the Coeur d'Alene mining district, one of the world's richest silver-lead-zinc mining regions.
county_metadata.research_tips: Shoshone County Recorder in Wallace holds deed records from 1864, among the oldest in Idaho. The Coeur d'Alene mining district is one of the world's greatest silver, lead, and zinc producing regions — Bunker Hill and Sullivan Mine, Hecla Mining Company, Sunshine Mine, and dozens of other operations generated employment records, payroll ledgers, and union records at ISHS, the University of Idaho Library, and the Wallace District Mining Museum. The 1892 and 1899 labor wars between miners and mine owners produced extensive court records, military intervention records at NARA, and newspaper coverage. Wallace, Kellogg, Wardner, Mullan, and Burke were mining towns attracting Irish, Cornish, Finnish, Italian, and Scandinavian immigrants; ethnic church records and fraternal organization records document these communities. The 1910 Big Blowup wildfire devastated parts of the county; fire survivor records at the Forest Service. The Cataldo Mission (1850s) is Idaho's oldest standing building.
